description: state of the United States of America
description_wiki: Mississippi (  MISS-iss-IP-ee) is a state in the Southeastern and Deep South regions of the United States.

description: American novelist, literary critic, scholar and writer (1914-1994)
description_wiki: Ralph Waldo Ellison (March 1, 1913 – April 16, 1994) was an American writer, literary critic, and scholar best known for his novel Invisible Man, which won the National Book Award in 1953.
The Ku Klux Klan (KKK; ), sometimes referred to as the Klan, is an American Protestant-led white supremacist and far-right hate group.

description: neighborhood in Manhattan, New York City, New York, United States
description_wiki: Harlem is a neighborhood in Upper Manhattan, New York City.

description: area of the city of Chicago, Illinois, USA
description_wiki: The South Side is one of the three major geographical "sides" of the city of Chicago, Illinois, United States.

description: 1960 non-violent protests in the United States
description_wiki: The Greensboro sit-ins were a series of nonviolent protests in February to July 1960, primarily in the Woolworth store—now the International Civil Rights Center and Museum—in Greensboro, North Carolina, which led to the F. W. Woolworth Company department store chain removing its policy of racial segregation in the Southern United States.

description: boycott in Florida to end racial segregation
description_wiki: The Tallahassee bus boycott was a political and social protest campaign against the policy of racial segregation on the public transit system of Tallahassee, Florida.

description: German Jewish Existentialist philosopher and theologian (1878–1965)
description_wiki: Martin Buber (pronounced [ˈmaʁtiːn̩ ˈbuːbɐ] ; Hebrew: מרטין בובר, Yiddish: מארטין בובער; 8 February 1878 – 13 June 1965) was an Austrian-Israeli philosopher best known for his philosophy of dialogue, a form of existentialism centered on the distinction between the I–Thou relationship and the I–It relationship.

Mohandas Karamchand Gandhi (; GAHN-dee; 2 October 1869 – 30 January 1948) was an Indian lawyer, anti-colonial nationalist and political ethicist who employed nonviolent resistance to lead the successful campaign for India's independence from British rule, and to later inspire movements for civil rights and freedom across the world.
Henry David Thoreau (born David Henry Thoreau; July 12, 1817 – May 6, 1862) was an American naturalist, essayist, poet, and philosopher.

description: American Baptist minister and civil rights leader (1929–1968)

description_wiki: Franny and Zooey is a book by American author J. D. Salinger which comprises his short story "Franny" and novella Zooey .
Albert Camus ( kam-OO, French: [albɛʁ kamy] ; 7 November 1913 – 4 January 1960) was a French philosopher, novelist, author, dramatist, journalist, world federalist, and political activist.

description: American pharmacy and convenience store chain
description_wiki: Walgreens is an American pharmacy store chain headquartered in Deerfield, Illinois.

description: defunct chain of five and dime stores located in the United States

description_wiki: The Congress of Racial Equality (CORE) is an African-American civil rights organization in the United States that played a pivotal role for African Americans in the civil rights movement.
Waiting for Godot (  GOD-oh or   gə-DOH) is a play by Irish author Samuel Beckett.

description: country located primarily in North America
description_wiki: The United States of America (USA), also known as the United States (U.S.) or America, is a country primarily located in North America.
